coredata.dc:title: Single step synthesis of β -cyclodextrin-sericin based nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots for visual and optical detection of Riboflavin via Inner Filter Effect

coredata.dc:description: A simple one-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their 2D analogs has been done by incorporating sericin into the β -cyclodextrin network for the visual, electrical, and optical sensing of Riboflavin (RFV). The as-prepared zero-dimensional nanoprobe exhibits blue-colored luminescence emitting at 424 nm, which undergoes a successive quenching phenomenon in the presence of RFV with the generation of a new peak at 532 nm. The two peaks maintain a synergistic relationship that depends linearly on the concentration of RFV. Similar results have been observed for artificial urine and human blood serum spiked with RFV. The corresponding detection limits are 36, 13, and 75 nM for aqueous media, artificial urine, and human blood serum, respectively. Moreover, there appears to be a gradual change in fluorescence color from blue to yellow–green due to the inherent yellow–green fluorescence of RFV. Further, the fluorescence intensity is found to decline with the gradual increase in temperature from 15 to 70 °C. Furthermore, the electrical conductivity of the probe increases with RFV addition.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs showing aqueous solution of SER, BCD, SBCDS, and RFV in visible (a) and UV light (b).
UV–visible (a) and Raman spectra of SBCDS (c); FTIR spectra (b) and XRD pattern of SER, β CD, & SBCDS (d).
XPS spectra of SBCDS; C1s (a), O1s (b), and N1s (c) and X-ray photoelectron survey spectra (d).

SEM images of SBCDS sheets (a) and Dots over sheets (b); SEM EDX spectra showing the percentage weightage of different atoms present in SBCDS (c); AFM images of SBCDS sheet along with its magnified image and height profile as insets (d), and SBCDS dots with the height profile of one dot as inset (e).

TEM images of SBCDS at two different magnifications (a & b) and its SAED pattern (c); SBCDS after 2 h of sonication at two different magnifications (d & e) and its SAED pattern (f); SBCDS after 8 h sonication at five different magnifications (g, h, i, j,& k) and its SAED pattern (l).
Emission (a) and Excitation (b) of SBCDS at different wavelengths.
Selectivity (a) and Interference study (b) of SBCDS.

Digital photograph (a), Photoluminescence spectra of ratiometric fluorescence quenching of SBCDS with increasing concentration of RFV (b), ratio of PL intensities at two different wavelengths (c) & absolute change in PL intensity with concentration (d); Benesi–Hildebrandt plot (e), and Stern–Volmer plot (f).
Overlap spectra (a); UV–visible absorption spectra of SBCDS with an increasing amount of RFV (b); and Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of SBCDS (c) and SBCDS+RFV (d).
Comparative study of the present sensor with other fluorescence and electrochemical sensors in aqueous.

head.ce:title: Single step synthesis of β -cyclodextrin-sericin based nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots for visual and optical detection of Riboflavin via Inner Filter Effect

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple one-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their 2D analogs has been done by incorporating sericin into the β -cyclodextrin network for the visual, electrical, and optical sensing of Riboflavin (RFV). The as-prepared zero-dimensional nanoprobe exhibits blue-colored luminescence emitting at 424 nm, which undergoes a successive quenching phenomenon in the presence of RFV with the generation of a new peak at 532 nm. The two peaks maintain a synergistic relationship that depends linearly on the concentration of RFV. Similar results have been observed for artificial urine and human blood serum spiked with RFV. The corresponding detection limits are 36, 13, and 75 nM for aqueous media, artificial urine, and human blood serum, respectively. Moreover, there appears to be a gradual change in fluorescence color from blue to yellow–green due to the inherent yellow–green fluorescence of RFV. Further, the fluorescence intensity is found to decline with the gradual increase in temperature from 15 to 70 °C. Furthermore, the electrical conductivity of the probe increases with RFV addition.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A poor diet can lead to the deficiency of several vitamins including Riboflavin. Riboflavin, also called Vitamin B2, is a water-soluble vitamin essential for food metabolism, nutrient absorption, tissue maintenance, enzyme and redox reactions, and various physiological effects [1]. The small amount of RFV produced by the gut bacteria is not sufficient to meet dietary needs; nevertheless, it is naturally present in some foods, and can be added to foods and consumed as supplements. Nonetheless, boiling foods can reduce twice the amount of RFV in them [2,3]. Moreover, RFV deficiency is caused by its continuous excretion from the body and also by the inability of the intestine to absorb it properly [1]. Its deficiency can cause various physiological disorders such as growth retardation, eye lesions, skin conditions, metabolic disorders, and so on. The excessive intake of RFV can cause itching, nosebleeds, tingling, etc.; however, a suitable upper limit has not been established, as the gut can absorb the required amount and discard the excess through urine [2,3]. Furthermore, recent studies have demonstrated RFV as a fruit and vegetable preservative [1]. Therefore, the detection of RFV is essential for food safety, supplement quality, and clinical diagnosis. There are different methods of detection among which the qualitative colorimetric way is the easiest and fastest but it is less reliable. However, the fluorescence-based method is both simple and reliable [4,5].

Fluorescent materials such as semiconductor quantum dots are prerequisites for demonstrating optical changes with analyte interaction [6]. Compared to quantum dots like CdSe, WO3–x, and CdS, the carbon-based ones exhibit superior photostability, low toxicity, and better biocompatibility. They exhibit high chemical stability, good conductivity and photoluminescence behavior, and broadband optical absorption [7].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a subset of CQDs, are small graphene sheets with lateral dimensions less than 10 nm with less than 10 graphene layers forming the final particle. GQDs exhibit physical and chemical properties like those of graphene. Because of the unique properties, they display better dispersibility, biocompatibility, photoelectric behavior, low intrinsic toxicity; paving their way for a broad range of applications including sensing [8,9].

There are different methods of preparation of quantum dots, which are broadly classified as top-down and bottom-up approaches; however, the bottom-up methods such as hydrothermal, soft-template, metal-catalyzed, etc. have been preferably employed because of their convenience, efficiency, less defect, controlled size, and shape, and so on. The hydrothermal method produces good quality and highly pure quantum dots under easily controlled experimental conditions [10,11].

There are several carbon precursors like citric acid, fumaronitrile, and l-glutamic acid deployed for the synthesis of N-GQDs by hydrothermal method. Some other precursors such as graphene or graphite oxide, Norepinephrine, glucose, glucosamine, Sodium citrate, and 1,3,5-Tri-amino-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene have also been utilized using microwave, soft template, etc. methods [12]. However, β -cyclodextrin has not been utilized so far to the best of our knowledge.

Cyclodextrins are cyclic oligosaccharides comprising a macrocyclic ring of 6–8 glucose sub-units connected by α -1,4 glycosidic bonds [13]. Among the three varieties of cyclodextrins, the β -form is the most widely employed because of its various advantages, including its stable and inexpensive nature [14].

The proper development of graphene-based nanomaterials is required for the advancement of the following generation of smart biosensors. Hence, GQDs have been doped with different elements. Doping can alter the optical, electrical, and optoelectrical properties of the GQDs. Out of different atoms, nitrogen doping has been the earliest and most widely adopted because of its comparable atomic size to carbon and five valence electrons for bonding. Different nitrogen sources such as citric acid, urea, melamine, glucose, ammonia, ethylenediamine, and chitosan have been utilized to date; however, as far as we are aware, silk sericin has never been utilized to synthesize graphene quantum dots or even as a precursor to nitrogen for doping. Additionally, it is not used for riboflavin sensing to the best of our knowledge [15].

Silk is a natural protein extracted from silkworm cocoons of both mulberry and non-mulberry kinds. However, Bombyx mori mulberry silk has been utilized in this communication because of its easy availability and extensive research. The fibroin part of the cocoon has been employed for various textile and biomedical applications and the sericin is discarded as a byproduct of the textile industry. However, sericin is now being studied in several domains, primarily for biomedical purposes [16]. On account of this, we intend to test sericin in the biosensing sector to create nitrogen-doped GQDs.

This manuscript demonstrates the single-step one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of graphene quantum dots employing cyclodextrin and silk sericin as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The as-prepared fluorescent sample named “SBCDS” acts as the probe for the optical sensing of RFV in aqueous media as well as in artificial urine and human blood serum (HBS) spiked with RFV. Furthermore, color change-based visual detection has also been shown in aqueous media and using filter paper strips. Additionally, the electrical sensitivity of RFV has been examined.

β -cyclodextrin hydrate (MW
= ∼
1135), Human Blood Serum (HBS), and a dialysis bag of 12 KDa were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Bombyx mori silk cocoons were purchased from the Mangaldoi silk farm (Darang district, Assam, India). All interfering molecules and salts were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL). Other reagents used were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The dead larvae were removed from the inside of the dried silk cocoons and the shells were cut into pieces. 10 g of the cocoon pieces were boiled in 2 L of 0.02 M Na
2
CO
3
solution for 20 min at 100 °C to separate sericin and fibroin [17]. The liquid part containing sericin is concentrated, filtered, dialyzed, and lyophilized to obtain sericin powder.

A simple one-pot hydrothermal synthesis procedure was followed for the preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene sheets/dots. Briefly, 568 mg (0.01 M) of β CD and 300 mg of SER were dissolved separately in deionized water at 55 °C. The SER solution was added dropwise to the β CD solution and left under stirring for 2 h at 75 °C. The resultant mixture was then put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 100 mL and heated in an oven at 175 °C for 18 h. The mixture was then filtered to obtain N-doped graphene oxide sheets and further sonicated to form N-GQDs named SBCDS [18–20]. The process of sonication involves the use of high-energy acoustic waves to induce cavitation and vibration effects, leading to the breakdown of bulk materials into nano-sized particles [21]. The product obtained was kept at 4 °C for further analysis.

The experiment was done using β CD alone but the product achieved did not exhibit any fluorescence behavior. Hence, no further characterization or analysis of the product using β -cyclodextrin alone was done. Further, reports reveal the formation of colloidal carbon spheres from the hydrothermal treatment of β -cyclodextrin instead of graphene sheets [22]. Furthermore, the literature survey reveals that the employment of sericin alone produces carbon dots instead of graphene sheets or GQDs [23]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements, characterization, molecular docking and sensing methodologies
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the concentration of the prepared SBCDS, 1 mL of it was poured into a glass petri plate, dried, and weighed. The concentration is calculated by subtracting the weight of the empty glass plate from the sample loaded glass plate.

The FTIR spectroscopy was carried out using a Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrophotometer employing 64 scans. The UV–visible spectrophotometry was done with a Shimadzu 2600 UV–visible spectrophotometer. The crystallinity of the samples was estimated using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with a scan rate of 2 s per step with Cu K alpha radiation. The tube current, voltage, and angular range were kept at 40 mA, 40 kV, and 2 θ
= 5-65
∘
, respectively. The photoluminescence spectroscopic measurements were carried out using a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer with a 240 nm/s scan rate and a halogen lamp as the excitation source. The size and zeta potential of the samples were ascertained by employing a DLS analyzer (Malvern NanoZS90) system in a glass cuvette with a square aperture and zeta dip cell electrode. The TEM experiment was performed by drop-casting the sample on a 3 mm copper grid and analyzing it using the JEOL TEM-2100 model. The XPS analysis was done using an ESCALAB Xi+ system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd.). Here, Al K was utilized as the X-ray source, employing an excitation energy of 1486 eV. The Raman spectroscopic measurements were done employing a 17 mW laser source with a wavelength of 633 nm using an HR model Horiba Jobin Vyon LabRam Laser micro Raman spectroscopic system. The morphology of the prepared samples, along with the elemental analysis through Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was checked using a Scanning Electron Microscope (Model at IASST: Zeiss ∑ igma VP). The surface morphology was further examined with an atomic force microscope (NTEGRA Prima, NT-MDT Technology) in a semi-contact mode using a silicon cantilever.
The electrical conductivity of the prepared sample was checked using an electrometer (Keithley 6517B) at room temperature.
The molecular docking study was performed using PyRx software with default parameters to see the interaction between β CD and sericin.

The fluorescence sensing experiments were carried out in a standard photoluminescence spectrophotometer using a 4 × 1 × 1 cm
3
standard quartz cuvette. The sample preparation was carried out by mixing 100 μ L of SBCDS into a solution containing 500 μ L PBS buffer (pH = 7.4), and 200 μ L of diluted real samples. The cuvette was then filled up with 3 mL of deionized water to which a varying amount of RFV was spiked and mixed thoroughly before taking the reading. The samples were excited at 340 nm wavelength and the corresponding emission spectra were recorded.

Various analytical techniques are reported for RFV detection such as liquid chromatography, mass spectroscopy, molecular imprinting, microbial & immunoassays, colorimetric and electrochemical methods including a few reports on fluorescence sensing [4]. Colorimetric visual detection is the simplest, most rapid and inexpensive method; however, its reliability is questioned [4,5]. The fluorescence method, along with its simplicity, is versatile and user-friendly, demonstrating better response time, multiplexing capability, and quality control. It has a greater number of donors and acceptors, shows reasonable prediction, reduces interference, and can be miniaturized [24–28]. Thus, it is thought to be a good idea to combine the visual detection method with fluorescence method. However, the literature survey reveals the employment of mostly electrochemical sensors for the detection of RFV as per our observation.
A comparative analysis of different sensor systems for the detection of RFV has been illustrated in Table 1 incorporating more of the fluorescent-based sensors.

As reported, N-doped GQDs have been used for the detection of molecules and ions like Thia-Cloprid, Homocysteine, Quercetin and
H
g
2 +
,
F
e
3 +
, respectively [43]. Further it has been employed for sensing 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol, Adenosine triphosphate, H
2
O
2
, pesticides, etc. including some other ions like
A
g
+
,
A
u
3 +
and
P
d
2 +
[44–46]. However, GQDs solely doped with nitrogen (N-GQDs) have not been reported for riboflavin sensing to the best of our knowledge.

Further, most of the RFV fluorescent sensors established so far are based on MOF (metal–organic frameworks) in which toxic heavy metals are utilized. Moreover, MOFs exhibit lower selectivity, signal loss, higher energy consumption, and a more complicated sensor configuration [47–50].

Du et al. [34] employed carbon nanodots showing comparable sensitivity to ours; however, GQDs exhibit high-speed electron transport with excellent conductivity. It is resistant to photobleaching and provides better surface grafting for better sensing and bioimaging performance [51,52]. Han et al. [32] synthesized a g-C3N4 nanosheet for sensing RFV; nonetheless, GQDs exhibit better sensitivity, selectivity, and stability as revealed from our result [45,53]. Mahajan et al. [31] prepared 9-anthradehdye derivative nanoparticles (AHNPs) for sensing RFV, nevertheless, GQDs demonstrate better optical properties and stability. It possesses excellent dispensability, better water solubility, lower intrinsic toxicity, and formidable biocompatibility. Further, our GQDs revealed better sensitivity compared to their study [8,54,55]. Overall, we can observe that we have obtained a LOD value in the comparable range compared to other reports, nevertheless, we have employed a simple, biocompatible, inexpensive material for the synthesis of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General information on the prepared graphene quantum dots (SBCDS)
ce:section.ce:para: The SBCDS product yield, concentration, and pH are found to be 96%, 4.5 mg/mL, and 5.8, respectively. The production yield we achieved is comparatively higher as per the recent survey by Im et al. [12].
The as-prepared N-GQDs (SBCDS) solution was found to exhibit a long-term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation at room temperature.

It was checked that SBCDS exhibited blue-colored fluorescence under UV light of 320–400 nm. Figs. 1(a) and (b) display the digital photographs of the aqueous solutions of SER, β CD, SBCDS, and RFV under visible and UV lights, respectively. It is visible that the prepared material “SBCDS” exhibits blue-colored fluorescence and the analyte RFV displays its well-known intense yellow–green colored fluorescence [56].

Fig. 2(a) displays the UV–visible spectra of the prepared SBCDS, along with its digital photo as an inset. The spectra indicate the formation of GQDs, exhibiting a strong absorbance peak at a wavelength of 223 nm and a comparatively weak shoulder peak at 264 nm attributed to the π – π * transition of the aromatic sp
2
domain of the CC bond. Further, a band can be observed in the wavelength range from 307 to 350 nm, ascribed to the n– π * transition of heteroatom-containing bonds such as CO/CN, characteristic of the polyaromatic structure [57,58].

The FTIR spectra of the samples are presented in Fig. 2(b). The spectrum of SER displayed a broad peak at 3277 cm−1, characteristic of secondary amide A, which designates the free and bonded absorptions of OH and NH stretching vibrations. The small hump at around 3056 cm−1 might be the characteristic of amide B arising because of the first overtone of amide II vibration originating from Fermi resonance [59]. The sharp peaks at 1645 and 1524 cm−1 might indicate amide I (CO stretching absorption or vibration) and amide II (NH bending and CN stretching), respectively. The single sharp peak at 1395 appears due to the CO stretching frequency. The peak at 1242 cm−1 appears because of amide III [60]. β CD displays characteristic peaks at 3282 cm−1 (OH stretching), 2921 cm−1 (asymmetric CH stretching), 1642 cm−1 (OH bending of H2O molecules), 1152 cm−1 (glucosidic stretching), 1077 cm−1 (CC stretching), 1016 cm−1 (CO C), and 856 cm−1 ( α -glycosidiclinking) [61–63]. In the spectrum of SBCDS, it can be observed that the small characteristic peak of β CD meant for the alpha-glycosidic bond at around 856 cm−1 disappeared and that at around 1016 & 997 cm−1 shifted to 1028 and 1005 cm−1, respectively, because of the change in CO stretching designates the breakdown of the alpha glycosidic bond. The CC bond of β CD maintained its integrity throughout the process, as shown by the emergence of its distinctive peak at 1077 cm−1 in SBCDS. The characteristic amide III peak of SER at 1242 cm−1 has reduced its intensity, signifying the complete incorporation or doping of amide III into β CD. The appearance of an intense peak at 1402 cm−1 designates the CO stretching frequency. Further shifting of the amide I (CH stretching) peak of SER from 1645 to 1660 cm−1 and the amide II (NH bending and CN stretching) peak from 1524 to 1583 cm−1 can be witnessed in SBCDS. Moreover, a new hump at 1460 cm−1 can also be observed, indicating the introduction of the aromatic double bond character of CC, which might specify the benzene ring of graphene.

The formation of graphitic carbon sheets was further analyzed with the help of Raman spectroscopy. SBCDS exhibits three characteristic bands at 1309, 1491, and 1625 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 2(c). The band at 1309 cm−1 originated due to the presence of sp3 carbon atoms and is referred to as the D band, while the band at 1625 cm−1 is considered the G band, usually assigned to the E 2 g mode related to the inplanar vibration of sp2 carbon atoms. The intensity ratio of these two bands (ID/IG) is used to determine the defect present in the 2D graphitic network. We have evaluated the ID/IG ratio for SBCDS and found it to be 0.56, which can be correlated to the graphene oxide synthesized from Hummer’s method [64]. The low value of this ratio indicates the existence of less disorder, which emphasizes the generation of electron sp2 domains due to the doping of amidic nitrogen on the graphitic network. The broad intense peak at 1491 cm−1 originated from the β -sheets of the silk sericin structure which slightly shifted due to the incorporation of β -CD [65]. Moreover, the other peaks are the characteristic peaks of the sericin counterpart, as reported in the literature [66–68].

The XRD patterns of the samples are portrayed in Fig. 2(d). It can be observed that SER exhibits a broad peak at around 21.5°, while β CD shows several sharp peaks, designating the higher crystallinity in β CD compared to SER. SBCDS displayed a broad peak centered at around 22.4° (2 θ ) corresponding to the interlayer spacing of around 0.39 nm. The peak may represent the (002) graphitic plane or surface defects or reduced oxygen formed due to the doping process. The high temperature and pressure conditions in the hydrothermal process might lead to disordered stacking and the creation of more active sites on the edges of the dots [69,70].

XPS analysis of the prepared SBCDS has been done to estimate the chemical bonding and elemental composition, as portrayed in Fig. 3. The C1s spectrum of SBCDS [ Fig. 3(a)] displays three peaks at 284.55, 285.34, and 287.98 eV, corresponding to CH/CC, CN (carbonyl), and NCO (amide carbon), respectively. The O1s spectrum [Fig. 3(b)] depicts two peaks at 531.06 and 532.47 eV assigned for CO and CO, respectively. Similarly, N1’s spectrum [Fig. 3(c)] portrays bands at 398.35, 399.78, and 401.34 eV characteristic of CN, NH, and CN, respectively. The comparative atomic % spectra for C, N, and O are shown in Fig. 3(d), including the quantitative value in tabulated form [71,72].

The SEM microphotographs of the prepared SBCDS before and after sonication are portrayed in Figs. 4(a) and (b), respectively. The morphology demonstrated the formation of sheets initially; however, on further sonication, there appeared as dots on the sheets [73]. The long lines in the SEM image of sheets signify the breaking of 2D sheets into small pieces. The elemental composition of the prepared SBCDS is further analyzed using an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) study, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The SEM-EDX analysis identified carbon, nitrogen and oxygen peaks as the major peaks. However, the non-assigned observed peaks at around 1.1, 1.8, and 2.2 are for sodium, silicon, and gold, respectively. The Si peak appeared because of the use of a glass substrate on which the probe was drop-casted for the SEM-EDX analysis. The Au peak is seen because of its use as the coating material in SEM. The Na peak arouses due to the use of Na2CO3 during the isolation of sericin from the silk cocoons. Since C, N and O are the concerned elements, these three elements were chosen to display while doing the SEM-EDX study. The obtained data demonstrated the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the graphene sheet. Fig. 4(d) displays the corresponding AFM images of SBCDS sheets (having dots over them), along with a magnified image of them (on the left) and height profiles (on the right) as insets. Further, the prepared dots and the height profile of one dot as an inset are displayed in Fig. 4(e). The AFM images revealed similar sheet and dot structures of heights around 45 nm and 4.5 nm, respectively [74].

The TEM images of the prepared SBCDS sheets and quantum dots are displayed in Fig. 5. SBCDS demonstrated large, thin sheets, as displayed in Figs. 5(a) & (b) at two different magnifications (500 & 200 nm), respectively. After 2 h of sonication, the appearance of dots on the sheets can be observed as shown in Figs. 5(d) and (e) at 500 and 100 nm magnifications, respectively. Finally, the sheets turned into dots after 6 h of sonication, as manifested in Fig. 5(g), (h), (i), (j), & (k) at magnifications of 50, 20, 10, 5, & 2 nm, respectively. The images confirmed the formation of around 4–8 nm-sized dots. The SAED pattern of the sheets [Fig. 5(c) & (f)] and corresponding dots [Fig. 5(l)] revealed its amorphous and semicrystalline natures, respectively, as understood by the non-existence and the appearance of some diffraction spots around the ring in the case of sheets and dots, respectively. Moreover, fringes can also be observed in the zoomed-in image at 2 nm magnification. The d-spacing value is checked and found to be 0.35 nm. Further, FFT analysis [Fig. 9 in the supplementary file] has also been done to evaluate the d-spacing value from which portrayed a similar number, i.e. 0.346 nm [75–77].

A molecular docking study was performed to check the interaction between β CD and sericin. It has been found that the amino acids “serine, glycine, and threonine” of sericin have interacted with the OH groups of β CD. The details of the study has been provided in Section 1.1 of the supplementary information (SI).

The photoluminescence emission spectra of SBCDS were recorded at different wavelengths ranging from 300 to 350 nm, and are displayed in Fig. 6(a). The maximum emission was observed at the 340 nm wavelength. Fig. 6(b) displays the typical fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. It was found that the emission was maximal at an excitation wavelength of 424 nm.
The photoluminescence emission spectra of SBCDS sheet and dot forms at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm have been compared, as shown in Fig. 5(a) in the SI.

A number of analytes, namely Tris[4-diethylaminophenyl]amine, Paromomycin sulfate, Ethambutol dihydrochloride, Vancomycin HCl, Cholesterol, Thiamphenicol, Cephalexin hydrate, Ivermectin, Isoniazid, Hydroquinone, Cefadroxil hydrate, Penicillin G. Sodium salt, G-418 sulfate, Cephalothin Sodium salt, Chloramphenicol, Histamine hydrochloride, Kanamycin monosulphate, Bromothymol blue, E-caprolactone, Palladium chloride, FOMO-6D-tryptophan, sulfamethoxazole, Dopamine hydrochloride, Azaerythromycin, D-Biotin, Neomycin sulfate, Curcumin, Coumarin, S-(-)-Tryptophan, Testesterone, Progesterone, and Fmoc-L-Tryptophan, each with a 0.001M concentration, was investigated for their effect on the probe solution (SBCDS), as displayed in Fig. 7(a).
It is visible from the figure that out of all these analytes checked, only Riboflavin could successfully quench SBCDS to a considerable extent compared to others.

The selectivity of SBCDS was further scrutinized for some molecules resembling similar functional groups to Riboflavin, such as glucose, aspartic acid, L-Alanine, L-Cysteine, Urea, Thiamine, L-Asoline, Ascorbic acid, Fructose, Vitamin B12, CaCl2, KCl, CuSO4, MgSO4, Folic acid, NaCl, K
+
, and FeCl3 as shown in Fig. 7(b). It can be observed that none of the above-mentioned molecules interfered in the fluorescence quenching of SBCDS except RFV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation in the fluorescence behavior of SBCDS sheets and dots
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of the sheet and dot structures of SBCDS have been compared, as shown in the supplementary information in Fig. 5(a). It can be observed that, irrespective of the distinct morphologies, the maximum emission is observed at the same wavelength for both the sheets and dots; however, the fluorescence emission intensity for the dots is comparatively higher than the sheet forms due to the well-known “quantum confinement effect” of zero-dimensional quantum dots [78]. Hence, further experiments are done with SBCDS dots. Although both the morphologies have different photoluminescence properties, the overall quenching efficiency of the sensor remains unaffected [Fig. 5 (b) in SI].

Fig. 8(a) displays a digital photograph of sensing RFV in aqueous media. It can be observed that the blue-colored fluorescence gradually turns yellow with increasing concentrations of RFV. The visual detection can be further accessed on filter paper coated with SBCDS, the color of which gradually changes from blue to yellow, with an increasing molar concentration of the analyte as shown in section 1.2 in the SI.

The photoluminescence emission spectra of SBCDS in the presence of varying concentrations of Riboflavin (RFV) in aqueous media are displayed in Fig. 8(b). It can be observed that the position of the emission maxima of SBCDS was retained even after the addition of RFV; however, the emission maxima underwent a bathochromic shift from 424 to 532 nm on increasing the concentration of RFV. With the addition of a 1.4 μ M quantity of RFV to SBCDS, a considerable decrease in PL intensity can be noticed. Further, a successive decrease and a simultaneous similar increase in the PL intensity at I424 and I532, respectively, can be observed with the increase in RFV concentration.

The dependency of these two peaks on the analyte (RFV) concentration can be determined by calculating the I424/I532 ratio and plotting it against the analyte concentration, as demonstrated in Fig. 8(c). From the calibration curve drawn [Fig. 8(d)], the limit of detection is estimated by using the formula (3.3*RSD)/slope, and it is found to be 0.036 μ M. The extent of bonding between SBCDS and RFV can be illustrated in terms of the binding constants determined by the Benesi–Hildebrandt relationship. This relationship can be expressed in terms of fluorescence intensity using the following equation:

Here, I0, I, and I1 are the emission intensities of the probe in the absence of, at intermediate state, and infinite concentration of RFV, respectively. The plot of 1/ ((I0- I)) Vs. 1/([RFV]) displayed in [Fig. 8(e)] gives a straight line indicating the 1:1 complexation and the slope gives the binding constant K’ which is found to be 0.0278 [79]. Fig. 8(f) portrays the Stern–Volmer plot demonstrating the way of interaction between SBCDS and RFV. According to the literature, the slope of the straight-line equation of the Stern–Volmer plot is called the Stern–Volmer constant or quenching constant which is found to be 0.0288 [80].
The quantum yield of SBCDS is found to be about 30%, which reduces to around 26% after the addition of 40 μ L of RFV.
The interaction of SBCDS (probe) with RFV (analyte) can be further estimated from the zeta potential measurements of the probe, analyte, and their mixture, as shown in Fig. 4 in the SI.

The sensitivity of SBCDS was further examined in artificial urine (AU) and human blood serum (HBS) spiked with RFV. A similar quenching effect, like that in the aqueous medium, has been observed. The details of the study have been provided in Section 1.5 of the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on the PL spectra of SBCDS
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the PL emission spectra of SBCDS as well as SBCDS + RFV has been checked, as illustrated in Fig. 7 in Section 1.5 of the SI. The PL spectra demonstrated a decrease in PL intensity with the increase in temperature from 15 to 70 °C in both cases.

Fluorescence quenching is governed by different mechanistic pathways such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), Forster resonance energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and the Inner Filter Effect (IFE). To interpret the probable mechanism, the extent of overlap between the absorption spectra of the RFV and the excitation/emission spectra of SBCDS were checked, as depicted in Fig. 9(a). The UV–visible spectra of RFV displayed its characteristic absorbance peaks at 223, 260, 373, and 445 nm, which overlapped significantly with the emission spectra of SBCDS at 424 nm. This result indicates FRET or IFE as the probable mechanism of quenching. To further confirm the result, Time-resolved Photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of SBCDS were analyzed alone [Fig. 9(c)] and in the presence of RFV [Fig. 9(d)]. There appeared to be a minute change in the lifetime of 5.74 ns (SBCDS) to 5.36 ns (SBCDS + RFV), which is considered negligible. Such constancy of lifetime is a characteristic behavior of the Inner Filter Effect (IFE). However, FRET is characterized by a decrease in lifetime in the excited state. Moreover, the IFE quenching might be static or dynamic, and hence, to evaluate, the quantitative absorption analysis of SBCDS in the presence of an increasing concentration of RFV is done as displayed in Fig. 9(b). The shifting of the absorbance peak of the probe to around 228 nm along with the appearance of two new peaks at around 370 and 445 corresponding to RFV indicates the occurrence of ground-state complexation. Tanwar et al. reported that a shift in the absorption peak of the probe indicates the form formation of a ground-state complex with Meisenheimer complex of the analyte and the appearance of a new absorption band corresponds to the formation of the intermediate Meisenheimer complex of the analyte. [81].

The electrical conductivity and sensitivity of the prepared probe towards RFV were checked using a chitosan membrane as the substrate. The probe demonstrated marginal conductivity; however, the conductivity drastically increased following RFV addition. The details of the procedure and results have been provided in Section 1.3 of the SI.

In this study, a simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly synthesis route is adopted to design novel carbon-based materials in two different dimensions. Firstly, the 2D-nitrogen-doped graphene oxide sheets are prepared by hydrothermal reactions between Bombyx mori silk sericin and β -cyclodextrin. These 2D sheets were further dissociated into zero-dimensional quantum dots with an average diameter of 5 nm, which exhibits excellent photoluminescence. The different colored fluorescence behaviors of the probe and analyte are utilized to construct a visual and an optical sensor for riboflavin. The fluorescence color of the probe solution as well as the probe-coated filter paper strips change from bright blue to yellow with increasing concentrations of RFV. Further, depending on the concentration of RFV added to the nanoprobe solution, the sensor undergoes a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. From these dependencies, we have evaluated the LOD and found it to be 36 nM using deionized water media, which is within the critical concentration limit of RFV (2–
18
μ g
/mL). The practical applicability of the sensor is further tested in two different real media, viz., artificial urine and human blood serum, for which the limit of detection (LOD) is found to be 13 nM and 75 nM, respectively. We have further assessed the molecular pathways involved in this quenching process using a variety of spectroscopic techniques and discovered that the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) plays a significant role. Further, the rise in conductivity of the probe after the addition of the analyte paves the way for designing an electrical sensor in the future. Furthermore, a smartphone-based gadget using probe-coated filter paper strips can also be developed for sensing RFV.
